text: Last class you ran the renewal meeting and reported every decision to the president.
Today the yearly review: How long has the partnership been working, and why continue?

Track record

We have been working together for three seasons.
The brand has been growing every year.
We have been building trust since the first contract.
Your logo has been reaching more fans for two years.
Dr. Patussi, thank you for the review. Tell me, why should we commit to a long-term deal now?
Because of our track record. We have been working together for three seasons, and the brand has been growing every single year.
That is true. But every partnership has problems. How reliable have you really been?
Very reliable. Since the first contract, we have been building trust. When there was a problem, we have been solving it together, like partners. We are proven, not a risk.
I agree, the relationship has been one of our best. So what is your case for the future?
Simple. We have been delivering results for three seasons, and the momentum is on our side. A long-term commitment now will sustain the growth for both of us.
The brand has been growing since the first contract.
We have been a reliable, proven partner.
We have been delivering results, and the momentum is on our side.
A long-term commitment will sustain the growth for both of us.

Hello again, Doctor Nilo, Sofia here from the FIFA Leadership in Football program office. Last time you ran the renewal meeting and reported every decision with care. Wonderful. Now comes a softer but very important skill: the partnership review. Once a year, you sit down with your sponsor and look back at the whole relationship. You ask, has this been good for both of us? Should we continue? To make this case well, you need the right words and the right grammar. So here is some advice. First, the words. A partnership is a long working relationship between two sides. A commitment is a serious promise to keep doing something. Trust is the belief that the other side will do what they say. A track record is the history of what you have actually done and achieved. When the work is ongoing, it is still happening now. When something is long-term, it lasts many years. A reliable partner is one you can always count on, and a proven partner is one who has shown results over time. Second, the grammar. To describe a relationship that started in the past and is still going, you use the present perfect continuous: have been plus the -ing form. You say, we have been working together for three seasons. The brand has been growing every year. We have been building trust since the first deal. This tense is perfect for a review, because it shows how long something has been happening and that it continues. Notice the two little words: for and since. You use for with a length of time, for three seasons, for two years. You use since with a starting point, since 2022, since the first contract. Third, the purpose. A partnership review is not just about the past. You use that strong track record to make the case for the future. You say, we have been delivering results for three seasons, so we are confident the next three will be even better. Look back to argue forward, Doctor. Show how long the partnership has been working, show that you have been a reliable and proven partner, and the case for continuing makes itself. Good luck.

Let me tell you about the annual partnership review with our main sponsor at Corinthians. Every year we meet to look back at the relationship and decide together if we should continue. This year I prepared carefully, because I wanted to make a strong case. I started with our track record. I said, we have been working together for three seasons now, and in that time the brand has been growing every single year. I had the numbers to prove it. I told them, your logo has been reaching more fans each season, and our shared campaigns have been winning awards. Then I talked about trust and commitment. I said, since the first contract, we have been building a real partnership, not just a deal. When there was a problem, you remember the late payments two seasons ago, we did not fight. We have been solving problems together, like partners. That is what makes us reliable. The sponsor's director was clearly pleased. She said the relationship had been one of their best, and she asked me a direct question. She said, why should we commit to a long-term deal now? So I used the track record to argue for the future. I said, we have been delivering results for three seasons, the brand has been growing, and the trust has been getting stronger. A long-term commitment now protects all of that. The momentum is on our side. She agreed. We have been partners for three seasons, and now we are planning the next three. The lesson is simple. A partnership review looks back to argue forward. Use the present perfect continuous to show how long the good work has been happening, prove you have been a reliable partner, and the case for a long-term commitment becomes obvious. Look back well, and the future signs itself.

We have been working together for three seasons.
The brand has been growing for two years.
We have been building trust since the first contract.
Our track record shows growth every season.
We have been a reliable partner every season.
A long-term commitment will help us sustain the growth.

Here is how to run a partnership review. First, open by looking back at the whole relationship. Then, show your track record with the present perfect continuous. After that, prove you have been a reliable and accountable partner. Next, point to the momentum and the growth. Finally, use the past to argue for a long-term commitment that will sustain it.
The brand has been growing since the first contract.
We have been a reliable, proven partner.
A long-term commitment will sustain the growth for both of us.
We have been delivering results, and the momentum is on our side.
